title: SNMPv3 Authentication Failures
spl:
index=network sourcetype IN ("snmp:auth","cisco:ios")
| search "authentication failure" OR "Unknown user name" OR "usmStatsUnknownUserNames"
| eval user=coalesce(user, user_name)
| stats count by src, device_name, user
| where count > 10
| sort -count
description: Auth failures indicate credential rotation gaps, brute force, or misconfigured collectors.
value: Auth failures indicate credential rotation gaps, brute force, or misconfigured collectors.
implementation: Forward device-side SNMPv3 error counters to Splunk. Alert on burst from single IP or new engine ID. Correlate with NetOps change tickets.
visualization: Table (top sources of auth failures), Timeline (failure bursts), Map (geo of source IPs if routed).
knownFalsePositives: Benign events during building tests, after-hours schedules, and vendor work—confirm against the BMS console before urgent response.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Device syslog, SNMP engine logs.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype="snmp:auth"` or `sourcetype="cisco:ios"` (SNMPv3 usmStats).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Forward device-side SNMPv3 error counters to Splunk. Alert on burst from single IP or new engine ID. Correlate with NetOps change tickets.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=network sourcetype IN ("snmp:auth","cisco:ios")
| search "authentication failure" OR "Unknown user name" OR "usmStatsUnknownUserNames"
| eval user=coalesce(user, user_name)
| stats count by src, device_name, user
| where count > 10
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**SNMPv3 Authentication Failures** — Auth failures indicate credential rotation gaps, brute force, or misconfigured collectors.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype="snmp:auth"` or `sourcetype="cisco:ios"` (SNMPv3 usmStats). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Device syslog, SNMP engine logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: network.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=network. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **user** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by src, device_name, user** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where count > 10` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search in a known window. Spot-check a few events against the BMS console (for example Niagara, Tridium, or Honeywell) and confirm zones, values, and alarms line up. Verify field names and that the right roles can search the index.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (top sources of auth failures), Timeline (failure bursts), Map (geo of source IPs if routed).
grandmaExplanation: We watch how your building systems behave. We help you know if something is off with device health the way a network-style poll sees it before harm, damage, or wasted effort piles up.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable (no CIM or Operational_Telemetry data model in this SPL).
